The account exhibits no economic purpose, functioning as a mere conduit for pass-through funds.
groundsForSuspicion: Account opened on 7 October 2022 and became active with large transactions only 8 days later, matching the 'newly opened account' indicator for pass-through typologies.

The balance dropped dramatically from ~£30,156 to £5,000 in under three hours on 15 October, indicating funds are not held but immediately transferred out.
No apparent economic or commercial reason for the high-volume, round-amount transfers; the activity appears designed to layer or quickly move funds.

claims.text: The account was opened on the same day as the flagged transactions, so there is no established profile to compare against for a mismatch.
All transactions are inbound from a single counterparty, which could represent a legitimate transfer such as a bulk payment, but no explanation is provided.

challenge.counterHypothesis: A customer who opened a new account specifically to receive a large, planned inflow from a single source (e.g., sale of property, inheritance), with documentation available.
challenge.distinguishingTestAssessment: The evidence does not satisfy the distinguishing test because: (1) the account was opened on the same day as the first transaction, which is consistent with a planned financial event; (2) all 12 inbound transactions come from a single counterparty, suggesting a structured but documented source of funds; (3) there is no indication that the customer failed to provide an explanation or that CDD was not updated—the profile is 'unknown' due to newness, not necessarily a mismatch; (4) the transactions are all inbound, which is less indicative of money laundering than outbound; (5) the amounts are consistent (approx £7,300 each), typical of installment payments from a sale or inheritance distribution. Without evidence that the customer cannot explain or document these transactions, a genuine change cannot be ruled out.

rebuttal.argument: The verifier's counter-hypothesis of a planned inflow into a new account is consistent with the evidence, as all transactions are from one counterparty and the account was opened immediately before the first transaction. Since there is no indication that the customer cannot explain or document these transactions, the activity does not meet the distinguishing test for KYC-01 profile mismatch.

reverdict.note: Triage conceded the challenge; disposition changed to dismiss.

typology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document; scam-mule guidance

strDraft.activitySummary: Between June 2 and June 13, 2026, Aisyah binti Kamal's personal account received five incoming transfers totaling 4,300 MYR from five unrelated individuals—Wong Siew Ling, Raj Kumar a/l Subramaniam, Lim Hui Min, Tan Mei Yee, and Goh Wei Liang—with no apparent connection to the account holder. On June 14, a single outgoing transfer of 2,000 MYR was made to Ilmu Educational Supplies Sdn Bhd, indicating consolidation and onward movement of the accumulated funds.
groundsForSuspicion: Multiple inbound credits from unrelated individuals over a short period (fan-in pattern)
Aggregated funds partially forwarded via a single outbound transfer to a business entity (fan-out/consolidation)
Senders have no apparent relationship to the personal account holder
Outbound transfer to a company is inconsistent with a personal account, suggesting possible misuse
Transactions exhibit indicators of layering or structuring through a personal account
Policy basis: FATF Recommendation 20 (Reporting of suspicious transactions); Bank Negara Malaysia AML/CFT and TFS for Financial Institutions (Policy Document).
strDraft.recommendedAction: Hold for analyst confirmation before filing — the verifier flagged this Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation) call; escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) only once the pattern is substantiated. Policy basis: FATF Recommendation 20 (Reporting of suspicious transactions); Bank Negara Malaysia AML/CFT and TFS for Financial Institutions (Policy Document).

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: On June 11, 2026, the account received MYR 95,000 from Gulf Star Remittance in Dubai, causing the balance to jump to MYR 95,300. Within hours, MYR 88,000 was transferred to Apex OTC Trading in the Cayman Islands, and the next day MYR 6,800 was withdrawn in cash, leaving only MYR 500.
groundsForSuspicion: Inbound wire of MYR 95,000 from a high-risk jurisdiction (Dubai) on June 11, 2026, was almost entirely drained via an outbound wire of MYR 88,000 to a Cayman Islands entity and a cash withdrawal of MYR 6,800 within 24 hours, leaving a balance of only MYR 500.
The account balance returned to near-zero after the rapid sequence, indicating no retention of funds and consistent with pass-through / rapid movement typology.
No economic purpose: declared as a student account with zero income, yet processed large international transfers and a cash withdrawal inconsistent with stated account use.
Policy basis: FATF Recommendation 20 (Reporting of suspicious transactions); Bank Negara Malaysia AML/CFT and TFS for Financial Institutions (Policy Document).
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Pass-through / Rapid Movement pattern. Policy basis: FATF Recommendation 20 (Reporting of suspicious transactions); Bank Negara Malaysia AML/CFT and TFS for Financial Institutions (Policy Document).

strDraft.activitySummary: Kevin Ng Jun Hao's account received five inbound transfers from unrelated individuals totaling MYR 24,500 within three hours. Almost all funds were immediately forwarded in a single MYR 24,000 transfer to an offshore trading company. The pattern is inconsistent with the account's young age and holder's profile, indicating potential layering.
groundsForSuspicion: Five unrelated senders transferred funds in quick succession, suggesting coordinated layering.
Aggregated funds (MYR 24,500) were consolidated and almost entirely forwarded offshore in one lump sum (MYR 24,000).
Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder, indicating potential third-party money movement.
The account, opened less than a month ago, shows high-velocity transactions inconsistent with expected personal usage.
The pattern mirrors the fan-in/fan-out consolidation typology often associated with money laundering.
Policy basis: FATF Recommendation 20 (Reporting of suspicious transactions); Bank Negara Malaysia AML/CFT and TFS for Financial Institutions (Policy Document).
strDraft.recommendedAction: File an STR with the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED) for the identified Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation) pattern. Policy basis: FATF Recommendation 20 (Reporting of suspicious transactions); Bank Negara Malaysia AML/CFT and TFS for Financial Institutions (Policy Document).
